title: 2026-06-17 2026 06 17 - HE (Part 3)
displayTitle: 2026-06-17 - Health Committee - 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Health [Part 3]
description: Wednesday 17 June 2026 | Health Committee 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Health Hearing of Evidence: Minister for Mental Health Hon Matt Doocey Health New Zealand—Te Whatu Ora Dr Dale Bramley, Chief Executive Jason Power, Acting Executive National Director - Planning, Funding and Outcomes Phil Grady, National Director, Mental Health and Addictions Pete Watson, Executive Regional Director, South Island Ministry of Health Audrey Sonerson, Director-General of Health Ruth Issac, Deputy Director-General, Strategy and Policy Group Andrew Old, Deputy Director-General, Public Health Agency and Mental Health Group
descriptionSummary: 2026/27 Estimates for Vote Health. Minister for Mental Health. Hon Matt Doocey

chunks.text: Thank you.

Welcome, Minister and officials. Thank you, Mr Chair. It's very good to have you all here. We have you here for half an hour today, so I'll invite you to make some brief introductory remarks, and we will hand over to questions from the committee. Thank you. Well, good afternoon. Thanks, Mr Chair and the Health Select Committee. Thanks for your time today to talk a number of things, but one I think is important to all of us as a country, and that's mental health.

I don't think I need to introduce too much. Audrey from the Ministry of Health and Dale from Health New Zealand, who you've already seen today, and also officials who I'm ably assisted by. I think the first thing I'd say is the government's mental health plan is delivering faster access to support, more frontline workers, and a better crisis response. It's really encouraging to see yesterday's report from the Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission that showed waiting times are coming down.

The workforce has increased since the government came into office. We've seen an increased number of people who are in mental health and addiction workforce education. And not only that, an increasing diversity of people who are choosing to work in this field. And I do want to take my time to acknowledge everyone who does the hard work at the front line of mental health and addiction services, whether they be in Health New Zealand or in our community NGO and Kiwi services as well. Not only did the report paint a favorable picture around recruitment, but

also waiting times were down, and access rates had increased as well. So look, that's a good position to be in to start today's conversation. To support that momentum, based on significant investment in Budget '25 and Budget 2026, we've invested an extra $100 million into three key areas. Faster access to support into maternal mental health. That's not only bolstering our specialist maternal services, but also rolling out peer support lived experience workers in our maternal mental health services. They're people who have been there before and will share their

experiences to help young moms struggling at a time of pregnancy and give them perinatal support. A better crisis response. We're opening up 20 extra beds. Currently across the system, on average, we have around 40 physical beds that are in place but have no funding to open. That's why we've made a significant investment, $51 million to open 20 beds.

That'll ensure people going through emergency department get timely access to inpatient support. And the final thing I'd raise, Mr Chair, before I pause is the investment, $28 million into the psychology assistant role. I'm really excited about that. We've worked hard in government on that new registration.

It's actually one of the first new health registrations in over a decade, investing for over 150 FTE at the frontline of mental health services. Really excited. Those students are starting in semester two at AUT and University of Canterbury, and they'll be in the workforce a year later. Thank you.

Over to you, Mr Chair and the committee. Thank you, Minister. First question, Tracey Martin, and then I'll come to Parewani. Thank you, Mr Chair, and thank you, Minister. Thank you for that update. I've got a couple of questions relating to what you've just said- Cool... but I want to go somewhere different to kick off. The Ombudsman has warned that people with complex mental health issues and intellectual disability are ending up in prison, because suitable specialist placements are not available.

Excuse me. Know that the Mason Clinic- Excuse me. Mm-hmm. Is that from this morning's discussion with the Ombudsman? No. You sure? I wasn't here- Okay... so I don't know what discussion you had. That's correct. Sorry. I just need-- We need to be careful here because there was a discussion from the Ombudsman which did state some things private to the- Oh, yes... private.

Sorry, Mr Chair. I wasn't privy to your discussions. I'm talking about my own information here. But the Mason Clinic funding is intact contingency, as you know. But it enables, well, funds enabling work rather than new beds. So can you talk us through any kind of additional...

It's a real gap when you talk about mental health. At the moment, we are essentially incarcerating people that the health system should be seeing because the health system isn't seeing them. And I just want to hear your thoughts on that and what- Sure... Budget 2026 hasn't done anything to address that gap. But I want to know where your thoughts are landing on what you will do next to address that gap.

Yeah, look, thank you for that question. And it is an important question around how we ensure better forensic mental health support. If you look at Budget '25, we invested $51 million into opening up 10 new forensic beds and eight step-down beds in the Waikato.

But it's also about building out the continuum, so that was funding more prison in-reach, FTE, as well as court liaison. We've continued that program of work in Budget '26 with an extra $36 million. Is that new capacity though? New beds Yes. So that will be four new beds as well as new step-down beds, new prison in-reach services, and new court liaison services as well.

Where I would say, though, is the crossover around intellectual disability and forensic, which comes under the responsibility of Louise Upston with Whaikaha. But Health New Zealand is contracted to deliver those beds. There is real pressure across the whole of our forensic system, including ID as well, and that's why we've got a program business case in front of Cabinet to look at the long-term planning of ID forensic services. We know there is a good mix of not only Health New Zealand services, but also our NGO and community services who provide that level of care as well.

But at the moment, there's at least 30 to 50 people at any one point in time, in a prison system who- Mm-hmm... but for the failings of the health system wouldn't be there. Do you agree that that's a problem? And are you committed to finding a solution?

I just need clarification because you're sort of going back between intellectual disability and forensic. Forensic. So you're talking forensic mental health- Mm... services. Well, that's the reality of the waiting list that we inherited when we came into office in late 2023. So that's why we did the work in our first year, and in Budget '25 announced $51 million. But I'd just point to you, $51 million in Budget '25 for 10 new forensic inpatient beds, and eight new step-down beds in the community.

They are quite expensive beds to fund- Mm... but we are committed to doing that. So not only have we funded more beds in Budget '25, continued to fund more beds in Budget '26, myself and Mark Mitchell, our Minister for Corrections, have got an active piece of work underway with both Corrections, and Health to look at how we better manage that wait list to ensure we've got the right prison in-reach services, as well as court liaison as well, and ensuring that we do build out beds in our forensic services.

So just finally, can I just confirm that Budget 2026, the Mason Clinic tag contingency, which we can't see the amount- Mm... is for enabling work? Yes. So that is CapEx. Not for new beds. And then we've got... It will be for new beds, and then we've got $36 million in OpEx. And I just see if Dale or Audrey want to add anything to that in the detail of that.

No. Nothing. That's right. Okay. Watch this space. Sure. Just a supplementary to that. Thank you all for being here. We of course, want to do some prevention before it gets to that level of care. Can you point to where in the budget you have got initiatives and programs around the prevention of people getting to that forensic mental health level?

You'll have to probably explain what you mean by that. So- Are you talking about in-prison mental health support? Yes. If you're talking about mental health support in prison- Mm-hmm... that's a budget that Mark Mitchell holds. So I'm talking about before they get to the prison system. So, that prevention funding for mental health, can you point to where in the budget you are focusing on the prevention- Yeah... getting there.

So if I start at a high level and step through it, and then you can ask me follow-up questions if I haven't got specifically to your point. When we came into office, the mental health and addiction ring-fence was 2.4 billion. Mm-hmm. After the increase in uplift with Budget '26, that will go close to 3 billion.

Part of my mental health target, setting New Zealand's first mental health targets, three of them are around access, one workforce, and one prevention and early intervention. So we have set a target that 25% of the mental health and addiction ring-fence goes into prevention and early intervention. Some might say, "Well, I work in a specialist service," which is part of your question, I think, "In the community with adults. How can I be seen as prevention and early intervention?"

But actually, when you take a philosophical approach is actually even at a specialist level, by intervening a lot earlier- Mm-hmm... and ensuring that we engage people potentially with lifelong enduring mental health issues, then we can keep them stable. They can better support themselves with their mental health issues and stay out of the criminal justice system.

Court liaisons is a good example of a program like that. Yeah. Can I have one more supplementary? One more supplementary. Thank you. No. Thank you, Minister. And yes, we are really pleased to see that increased funding in the vote. When we are looking at prevention, can you tell us how much of that is going to be in different areas?

So for example, with prevention, how much will be in youth or with seniors or in men's mental health? Yeah. That would probably take some considerable work to get into a- Postcare impression... health system across the country- Yeah... that spends $3 billion, how you would tag that for either male Versus female because, of course, when you look at the investment into our primary care mental health services, it's actually universal, so we wouldn't measure it by that.

But your point is valid about young people. That's why we do have specific young people's programs within primary care. It's why we do resource programs like Gumboot Friday to ensure that we have available, appropriate youth mental health support. But in fairness, if you look at the report released yesterday by the Mental Health and Wellbeing Commission- Mm... that talked about still the pressures of access around young people. What's really interesting in that, when you look at the Ministry of Youth Development survey, only

3.7% of young people said they couldn't access support because there wasn't an available appointment. Only 3.7. In fact, over a third reported they didn't know where to go, or they were too embarrassed to ask for help. So it shows we've still got to push through barriers of stigma and discrimination.

I get a good overview of our mental health system. There is still capacity in our youth services, but what it says to me, it's the type of services they might perceive as medicalized or adult, and we do need more youth specific mental health services that are more engaging for our young people. Go to your primary? Yes, please. Thank you.

It just follows on very nicely from this. So just recently, the new UNICEF statistics came out. It's a- I'll come to you next, Carmel ... out of 37 countries, and New Zealand was ranked 37 out of 37 for youth mental wellbeing. And I know that there has been that increase in funding.

Can you tell us how much of that will go towards a YOSS, the Youth One-Stop Shops? We know that there was-- We were both at the opening of the new event center at the wonderful Christchurch Youth Hub, and we know that there has been work done across Parliament under one umbrella.

And there's recommendations in there around how to best support our young people with- Yes... these YOSSES. Can you tell us what is being done in this budget, further to what you've just outlined, around picking up these tamariki who might have had support but aren't getting the further support that they're actually needing, so that they're not...

Yeah. We just want to look after our young people- Yeah. No, that's a-... and those Youth One-Stop Shops... valid question. Yeah. There's a lot to do in that space, and I acknowledge it's an area that we've got to work collectively on. And what I would raise is that I'm a big supporter of the YOSS model. Mm-hmm. And actively, at the moment, through a policy lens, we're going through an evaluation of Youth One-Stop Shop.

My hope that will inform future funding decisions around how we can grow that model as well. You asked for specific questions. I'd refer to the recent investment out of the Hauora Māori Appropriation, where we're significantly investing into youth crisis support- Mm... especially in the Northland area, new kaimahi roles based after a coroner's recommendations.

But I would say also is that when we look at young people in the sense of teenage years, we quite often say: What can we do in early intervention and prevention at that age? What we do know is we actually need to get into those young people and their families-... in the first 2,000 days, if we're really true to prevention and early intervention. So that's why we've announced the $20 million out of budget 2026 to look at how we scale up not only our specialist maternal mental health services, our peer support lived experience, but also more prevention

through community and NGO services, and a significant funding into the new bereavement pathway to support those parents who have sadly been bereaved by maybe loss. Thank you, Minister. I've just got two supplementaries. That's all right. One around the YOSSES, and then one, jumping to the pre and perinatal mental health, because you spoke about it there. So with the Youth One-Stop Shops, we're hearing around the country that they've got the teams there for work readiness.

So the funding is tagged to work readiness, but that there is a lack of funding for counseling for the need that is being shown there, and that there isn't sort of a streamline that goes from a YOSS in Rotorua compared to Christchurch. So with the readiness teams, it seems like those are well-funded, but it's the counselors that they are saying to us don't have enough funding. So within that $20 million, how will that be allocated? Work readiness, it's great.

We want young people to be- Yes... out there doing these things. But the counseling is where, at the Youth One-Stop Shops- Yes... is also important. Yeah, look, I can't announce any funding for Youth One-Stop Shops today. But around the pressure of youth counseling, what I'd also point out too is actually there is capacity within the Gumboot Friday contract of seeing 15,000 young people a year.

And when you look at the Youth Access and Choice program, there is definitely capacity within that. So that's part of joining up the system, so when people do have demand that they can't sustain, that they look to available services to support those young people in a time of need. Thank you, Minister. And just one more supplementary with the- No, that's all right ... the prenatal health. And thank you for- For looking at that and putting budget or vote into the pre- and postnatal health for the first 2,000 days of-

... tamaiti and whānau's lives. Because what we're hearing is that there are touchpoints throughout, say, a pregnant parent's life and a pregnant parent's pregnancy, and that they are not getting the care, and then they're getting to crisis level.

Mm. Yes. And then that is when all of the services come around. So what is going to be happening in those first touchpoints where people aren't getting to crisis level, and having to be put into institutionalized- Yeah... with a newborn- Yeah... which isn't ideal, obviously.

No, that's right, and that's why we do want to invest more into prevention and early intervention, specifically in the first 2,000 days. So it's not only bolstering our specialist services so mums and dads will have more timely access, so they don't have to wait till they get in crisis. But also, within that, there is a million-dollar community fund to support our community and NGO and iwi providers as well. So we're ensuring that we do build out along that continuum.

Because we do know the more resource we can get upstream, then we can prevent people needing crisis services, but more expensive crisis services. Mm. So it's better for the taxpayer and actually better for the individual themselves. All right. Minister. Thank you. We've got primaries from Carlos, and then back to Casey. Thank you. Thank you, Chair. Minister, last year as mental health packages, we actually spent a lot of funding to operate, or at least open at least six crisis recovery cafe.

Mm. So how is it work? Can you give us a little bit update, how many people have been using the services, and what is your future plan on this initiative? Yeah, it's part of the mental health plan that not only do we want faster access to support- Mm... but more frontline workers and a better crisis response.

What we do know with the use of our peer support lived experience workforce, that's a workforce that's ready to go. Mm. We can stand them up quite quickly. And it's a workforce that shows it gets real results. When I open the new peer support lived experience service in Wellington ED, one of their workers said it was quite timely that she was now there as a worker because she had been to ED herself in a time of crisis. I think the ability to have someone alongside of you who's been there before- Mm... will make a real difference. Not only that, when you think about the peers in

ED and also the crisis recovery cafes that are peer led as well. We're rolling out peer support lived experience workers and eating disorder services now in maternal mental health service as well. They'll be all joined up. What we do know with the crisis recovery cafes, quite often they are going to pick people up from ED.

If you think about it, when you're in a crisis, the last place you want is a brightly lit, noisy place with a lot of people. And so what they are getting in a crisis recovery cafe is a non-clinical service, something that is more suited to their needs, getting the support they need, and I think that'll be a real game changer.

Mm. So I'll be expecting more crisis response cafe being open in the next 12 months? Yes, very much so. Recently been down in Christchurch, where we opened the first crisis recovery cafe there. Dunedin after that, and we're looking to roll them out across the country.

Thank you. Tracey then Jenny. Facilities, and let's start with Hillmorton, something we're both familiar with. And for some time we've been hearing about the plight of staff at Hillmorton. The new facility is now not expected to open until late 2027, I believe, but correct me if I'm wrong.

Will all of the 80 beds be staffed and funded from day one? Yeah, we'll look to transition into that. If you look at the experience of Palmerston North, even the Mason Clinic, it does take time to transfer patients. Of course, when you think about the models of care, for the staff, it's ensure that they are prepared, and they go through training for a new facility as well.

So for me, it's not necessarily the speed we do something. It is the safety we do that as well. Of course, we will need beds open that match the number of patients that will transfer into that, and we're committed to ensuring that we have a better level of facilities. I do want to acknowledge the last Labor government that started off that significant investment into new mental health facilities as well.

They are quite literally, I think, a game changer when you think of the very tired state that people in mental health inpatient facilities had to endure- Mm... when they were there to get well. Mm. And thankfully now they go to new facilities that are therapeutic.

So we're really committed to that. We know there's real pressure across system, but ensuring that we get the right beds in the right place. Okay. So in regards then to the other builds, are there any other builds or upgrades that have also been delayed? And have you got any indication via this budget 2026 process about when those kind of opening dates will be?

Yeah, none that I'm aware of, but I'll open it up whether Dale or Phil Bridge got anything to offer there No. Not aware of any So you're not aware of any other delays? Mm-hmm. Okay. You also-- We haven't really touched on addiction services, and the three-week referral date. You know, the- Yes... to be seen within- Three-week target, yes... three-week target.

Yes. So at the moment, only around about three quarters, I see, of addiction referrals are being seen within that three-week timeframe, which can be worse than other mental health referrals. So what in Budget 2026 do you think is going to address that gap?

Yeah. Well, sometimes it's not actually more money. Mm. And one of the reasons I set the first mental health and addiction targets in New Zealand was to have, A, an expected level of service that I thought Kiwis wanted from their mental health and addiction service, but also that and a level of accountability.

And the reality is now that the level of discussions that we're having with executive regional directors responsible for each of the four regions around the mental health targets, I even met with them on Monday, is now we can push into the 20 health districts. We can look at data, Māori, Pasifika, Asian, rural and urban, and age groups. So yes, our expected level of service is 80%. Nationally for one week, we are at 84%, and specialists are 82. But I've always been very clear I'm not happy with the national average.

And when you look at the 20 health districts, there is variation, and you are quite rightly pointing out the variation for addictions as well. Mm. So part of the responsibility now for the executive regional directors is quarterly they prepare action plans and improvement plans that show how quarter on quarter they are going to increase the wait time so they meet those targets, and that's what I'm holding Health New Zealand to account for.

And you're still quite satisfied with wait times being the way to operationalize effectiveness and efficiency? Well, I'm no target fundamentalist. But actually what it does is put a line in the sand- Mm... that allows me to have a conversation with executive regional directors to say, "If you look at an area where at the moment, Capital Coast, how are you going to improve to ensure you get the targets?"

So for us, under this government, it's about pushing back local decision-making to the front line around service delivery- Mm... budgets and workforce. And I think it's actually going to be a real game changer around postcode lottery because for the first time, we can drill into specific communities and measure them against a national standard. Then my expectation is Health New Zealand works with not only their own services but NGO and community and iwi services to ensure there's services there that speeds up that access to that national standard.

Mm-hmm. Shane. Thank you, Minister. My question's around what's happening in the rural communities in terms of mental health support. Can you give us an overview of the work you've been doing in that area in light of we've just come off the back of field days, and some of us may have gone to the Rural Support Trust evening event, which was raising money, but also showcasing or highlighting a particularly grim story from a woman and her family and the impacts of suicide.

Yes, very much so. And we do know the pressures and need on our rural community- Mm... and our farming community specifically- Absolutely... and the pressures faced on farm as well. And like you, just to acknowledge the great work of our Rural Support Trust. Quite often volunteers, and that's why the government was keen to commit $3 million to support Rural Support Trust.

They can do great navigation work into our primary and specialist services. And the good thing, just following on from that last discussion, is having the targets and the ability to pull out rurality and urban. That's the first time under the geographical classification of health, the GCH, we can do that.

Which allows us in districts and regions to look at performance for rural communities that isn't masked by better performing urban areas as well. And that'll be a game changer. But what's quite interesting is rural and Māori quite often outperform other areas in those targets as well. So sometimes it does challenge to say- ...

"Absolutely, we're doing a good job in some areas." And when I talk to executive regional directors, they can learn from each other- Mm-hmm... about their areas that are performing well, and also the areas that are not performing as well. But there is real challenges when you think about the geographical isolation- Mm... that people do experience on farm.

And that's why the question before about, do you stand by your targets around efficiency in the best way? Well, the reason I think they are a good way of delivering performance is it's not for me to get on the shop floor. But by setting a clear guidance and expectation, when I go around the country, especially in rural communities, they're talking about things like telepsychiatry, singular intake systems. So they can come up with their solutions about how they ensure people in their communities get that care.

Might be a hybrid in person online or more online through Starlink. So it's great innovation out there. Right. Just final- Final... final. You mentioned in the budget $28 million for 150 FTE relating to a new role called psychology assistant. Can you just give us a brief look at what that role is and what will they do, and is it a graduate role through the education role?

Yeah. Look, we're really excited. The problem definition in New Zealand is that the reason why we weren't keeping up with workforce is, A, we didn't have a plan. Mm. So we published New Zealand's first mental health and addiction workforce plan in our first year in 2024 and tagged $44 million to it as well.

We've also committed to doubling the amount of clinical psychologists in their internship training, taking it from 40 to 80. We're currently sitting at 74 against a target of 70. And also increasing psychiatry by 50%, taking first year intake from 33 to 48. But what the data showed is that psychology is one of the most studied courses at university. But when people weren't getting one of those clinical psychology internships, they were disappearing from the field.

Right. So we created a one-year postgraduate, and AUT and University of Canterbury will be the first to open in the start of July. We also know there's considerable interest from other universities. And that's why we thought it was only right for those students going into class in July, that we give them a commitment that there is fully funded roles there for psychology assistant, scaling up to 150.

So when they graduate, they are going in. Also, I want to be clear because there was some criticism that we thought we were going to cannibalize clinical psychology roles within Health New Zealand to fund these, and that's why Budget '26 commitment shows we're sustaining clinical psychology funding, if not increasing it, and then new funding for psychology assistants.
Thank you, Minister. We are at time. Appreciate everyone- Thank you very much. Thanks, mate.
